own_review.issue: KDoc on the now-active expect declarations maxAge/maxStale/minFresh still references [TimeUnit.SECONDS], but java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit is JVM-only and not importable in commonMain — broken Dokka links on all three methods. Should read 'seconds precision' or reference [DurationUnit.SECONDS].

JVM Builder now exposes BOTH DurationUnit and TimeUnit overloads for maxAge/maxStale/minFresh — the public API surface is doubled, and multiplatform callers cannot write shared code against the TimeUnit variants. This is the design problem the maintainer flagged ('stuck doubling our methods'); a kotlin.time.Duration-based API or common alias would avoid it.
Newly added KDoc on the TimeUnit overloads duplicates the commonMain expect docs verbatim — doc maintenance duplication.
FORCE_CACHE remains commented out in commonMain even though this PR makes maxStale available in common code; it only needs maxStale(Int.MAX_VALUE, SECONDS) and could be promoted now.

Common tests only exercise DurationUnit.SECONDS; no cross-unit conversion, sub-second truncation, or Int.MAX_VALUE clamping coverage for the new common path (CacheControlJvmTest.kt covers those only for the JVM TimeUnit overloads).

NO-ISSUE verification note: toDuration(unit).inWholeSeconds saturates (out-of-range -> infinite -> Long.MAX_VALUE) then commonClampToInt caps at Int.MAX_VALUE, exactly matching old TimeUnit.toSeconds + clampToInt semantics. Kotlin is 1.6.10 so DurationUnit is a standalone enum on JVM — no platform declaration clash with the TimeUnit overloads. Any overflow-regression claim would be wrong.

points.point: commonMain KDoc references [TimeUnit.SECONDS] which cannot resolve on non-JVM targets; change to [DurationUnit.SECONDS]
points.note: Verified: TimeUnit is JVM-only, not imported in commonMain; broken links on all three methods.

points.point: Common test only exercises DurationUnit.SECONDS; JVM tests cover DAYS/MILLISECONDS but no non-JVM equivalent; add MINUTES/HOURS conversion test
points.note: Verified: CacheControlJvmTest.kt lines 174/184/204 cover DAYS, clamping, MILLISECONDS for TimeUnit only; common test uses only SECONDS.

points.point: commonClampToInt lacks underflow clamp; rename or add Int.MIN_VALUE branch
JVM TimeUnit overloads now delegate to commonClampToInt; 'verify bytecode resolves Integer.MAX_VALUE equivalently (this is fine)'
points.note: Vacuous self-answered observation; no issue raised.

points.point: KDoc could note the value is interpreted in the given DurationUnit before conversion

missed: JVM method-doubling design problem: dual TimeUnit/DurationUnit overloads double the API and block multiplatform callers (the human maintainer's sole comment)
FORCE_CACHE could now be promoted to commonMain since maxStale is available in common (left commented at CacheControl.kt:168)
exceeds: Broken [TimeUnit.SECONDS] KDoc links in commonMain (not raised by human)
Non-JVM DurationUnit conversion test-coverage gap (not raised by human)

points.point: Stale [TimeUnit.SECONDS] KDoc references in the common expect declarations; correctly notes the new JVM overload docs are fine since those genuinely take TimeUnit
points.note: Verified, and the JVM/common distinction is exactly right — most precise statement of this issue.

points.point: commonClampToInt omits negative clamping; 'no action needed' given require(>=0) guards

PR establishes a new multiplatform time-unit pattern: first kotlin.time usage in the codebase, diverging from the TimeUnit + java.time.Duration convention in OkHttpClient.Builder, with TimeUnit kept as a JVM-only supplementary overload
points.note: All factual claims verified (zero kotlin.time hits at base commit; OkHttpClient imports both java.time.Duration and TimeUnit). Substantive API-precedent observation adjacent to the maintainer's concern, though it stops short of flagging the doubling problem itself.

points.point: Test coverage minimal: no cross-unit conversion (MINUTES->60s), millisecond truncation (4999ms->4s), or overflow clamping tests for the DurationUnit path; JVM tests cover these for TimeUnit
points.note: Verified against CacheControlJvmTest.kt; concrete, correct test cases proposed.

missed: JVM method-doubling design problem as a *problem*: point 3 describes the dual-overload structure but never flags that the API is doubled and multiplatform callers are stuck (the human's actual concern)
FORCE_CACHE could now be promoted to commonMain since maxStale is available in common (left commented at CacheControl.kt:168)
exceeds: Broken KDoc links with correct common-vs-JVM scoping (not raised by human)
Verified-true kotlin.time precedent observation (not raised by human)
Concrete DurationUnit test-coverage gaps (not raised by human)

qualitative: Both candidates found the substantive broken [TimeUnit.SECONDS] KDoc links in commonMain and the non-JVM test-coverage gap, and neither fabricated anything — every absence claim (no non-JVM unit-conversion tests, first kotlin.time usage, OkHttpClient's TimeUnit/java.time.Duration convention) verified true against the base commit. Neither candidate made the false overflow-regression claim; v5 even implicitly and v7 explicitly treated the clamping as sound. v7 is the stronger review: its KDoc point correctly scopes common vs JVM docs, its test point gives concrete verified cases, and its kotlin.time-precedent observation is accurate and comes closest to the maintainer's method-doubling concern, though neither candidate actually articulates that the dual TimeUnit/DurationUnit overloads double the JVM API and leave multiplatform callers stuck. v5 pads with three zero-value points (an unreachable underflow nitpick, a vacuous self-answered bytecode note, and a doc-wording suggestion), diluting an otherwise accurate review. Both missed the FORCE_CACHE promotion opportunity that this PR unlocks.
